speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four-- the interview with Lillian Bielsky-Bell, December 1, 1996. So the Pole told you to come upstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: After 6 o'clock to him to the office-- he had a little office there. And-- but before I came to him, he came out. Because when they started to select the people-- for-- to take the people from work and put them-- line the-- line them up to go back to the ghetto, he came to me and he says, no! Go home. I don't need you. But I saw something is bothering him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next day, I came to work again. He called me outside. He said to me, your stepmother was hiding last night by me in the barn with other people. She want-- she gave me five dollar-- five ruble gold. She wanted me to keep you after 6:00 and I'll let you go alone home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the results of it, we both know. He said to me, but I'm appalled, he says. But I have a fatherly feeling. You are so young. Why should I take with my own hands, for five ruble gold and kill you? Since then, I said to myself-- I said, God, I was so early a orphan, why did you want to kill me if Hitler didn't kill me two weeks ago? I survived, and she wants to be my killer? Why can't you-- why should we be mad at the-- at the-- at the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I didn't say a word. I never told my father this story. Never.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he couldn't take it already. He saw that my suffering-- you know-- a lot of people in one room. Each one is bringing a piece of bread with-- through his teeth he's bringing-- until he gets it. You know, what-- what did they give us? Nothing. So you had to sell something from the house, or to take something and exchange with somebody. And it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the missus needed everything the best for her. If it was something in the house, she was the first one-- she and her son. Her son took a liking to me. That's true. Maybe this was the reason that she was afraid that something shouldn't come out of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This-- the-- the slaughter was May 8th, in the end of June. During June, when Tovia heard the whole story of what's going on--

speaker: interviewer
text: With?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with-- with the-- it was May 8th. He was-- in fact, he was in-- in-- he wasn't in the ghetto. But he was in Lida when this happened. And he sent somebody to tell us, and begged my father to get out from-- from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said, absolutely no. In the ghetto, we used to get together, you know, in somebody's yard at night, until-- until the time that we were allowed to in the ghetto, and sing the-- of-- oh, yeah-- each one of us belonged-- not each one. I didn't belong. My parents didn't allow me-- to some kind of a party, you know, like the Zionists, like the Betar, like-- and sing the songs from-- from once upon a time, and think what will be tomorrow? Go home and pray to God that tonight should nothing happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: One night-- it was in the end of June-- I came from one of my girlfriend. And I was cold. So she gave me her jacket to throw on me. And I came home, and I get undressed. And I got to bed. And I hear somebody is patting me on the shoulder. In my eyes, I see my father. He said that's the night that we are going out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, dad, you saw me. I just got undressed. Why today? Why not-- don't ask any questions. I decided then I want to be out. In her jacket-- and a-- that's what I could carry in my hand, because we had to climb under the fence of the-- the iron fence, you know, with the-- The barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the wiring. And on the other side, they had the dogs. But when we went out, it wasn't as bad as later on when they got smart and they saw that people were disappearing already. But still the crawl was maybe a good mile on the stomach-- me, my father, and my stepmother, and my stepbrother. And 1/2-- not halfway, but 10 kilometers from Lida, even less maybe, my-- Tovia was waiting for us. He took us out to a-- to a peasant. There, he made arrangement with his brother to wait a little farther in the woods for us. And they brought us to the group. There were 17 people with us. It was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was it who waited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asaela and Zu.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh-- oh, both brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to them in the woods, how many people were you all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 with us-- 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other people were not yet with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No! Everything started when we got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when it started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when it started. And everybody was amazed in the ghetto, because we disappeared, you know. And I had no intention-- God as my witness-- that I had no intention of going out. I didn't know nothing. I just threw a-- her jacket on me, because I was cold. It was a chilly night. But I never, in my farthest dream, didn't expect that my father will go out that night. He had no-- even with no stop, my-- my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very hard trip, because we had to crawl. The dogs were on the other side of the fence. It was very hard to crawl. But as soon we passed the danger of the dogs, with the-- with their-- with the-- people that was on-- on duty there, standing and watching, we-- we were saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father take anything with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the personal belongings, what he could carry. Nobody took nothing. We disappeared without saying boo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? Where did Tovia take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tovia took me as his-- to the family. There was only family members.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- the 17 people were only family members.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they? Where were they located?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came, they were in a little part of the woods already by-- by their hometowns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stankiewicz-- in this part.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were living in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, they used to live where-- by peasants. They still went in and out from the peasants. It was nothing organized. With our coming, then it started. Then he wrote a letter to his cousin in Nowogrodek, telling him enough to sit in the ghetto. Take young people and come to the woods. I don't-- and-- and every letter was the same thing. I don't promise you nothing, but I know that you'll be free, and you won't be behind iron bars. If you leave too, it'll-- it be-- it will be our luck. And seven first people came to-- strangers, not family.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Nowogrodek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was Mr. Frydberg. It was Mr. Berger. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --you know, you wrote down now, it came to me. Did I said to you Windisz? Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it came to me when I'm talking about that other people. So it came to me. It was two brothers. It was Judel Levin. It was Judel Bielski and his wife. It was-- it was seven people. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were among--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jankielewicz-- two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who were among the 17 that joined--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just family, just family-- brothers, sisters, children--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean Tovia's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of Tovia's family. They were connected with another family, because-- through marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the five of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the five of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the five of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four of us-- Sonia, our sister, wasn't with us. She was already by-- I told her. She was at the peasant farmer's house doing alteration--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and hiding there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was you, and your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the stepmother--

speaker: interviewee
text: --stepmother and step-- stepbrother.

speaker: interviewer
text: --stepbrother. And how about the stepmother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed May 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. OK. All right. So how did you live in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I live? For me it was everything you knew. First of all, she was bossing around a lot, you know, and-- Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tovia-- my stepmother. She was, you know, her sister was with Tovia. So she was on-- on the horse. So she bossed me around, and Tovia couldn't take it. He saw such a mischief. He saw such a-- you know, no-- what can I tell you-- no justice, that he couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he used to always bring me something to-- very warm from the-- from the farmers-- a pair of boots, or a pair of something, a sweater-- something warm. It's-- the-- there it was very cold in the winter-- and at night too. He took care of me. He could see the non-injustice that was done to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was-- he couldn't do nothing. He couldn't do nothing. He liked-- he loved me-- he loved me, and he loved his wife. And he was in between. And he didn't-- he could not make the choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- did you build a-- a shelter in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning, not in the beginning. In the beginning, we bought-- we made barracks, but on top of-- on the ground. Later on, we learned to do it into the ground, you know, hiding all the way in the ground. Because it was warmer, and it was more-- it was more-- as people started to come, there are all kind of people with different abilities. And they started to build and bring in new things to the-- to the group. So for the winter, there was different preparations already, and-- and you slowly, slowly, you learn how to live like a animal. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left in the end of June, 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my sister-in-law, Sonia, came in August, I think. This was after the seven people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my husband called somebody else that when he was 16 years old, they had a mutual uncle. It was their uncle and his uncle. So-- and he danced, when he was 16 years or 17 years, danced on their wedding. They had a daughter. And one daughter was killed in Nowogrodek during the action too. And one daughter was alive. And he wrote him a letter. I danced on your wedding, but I don't want you to dance on the grave. Send out harm. And the first thing, send out your-- your daughter to me. And my sister-in-law and this girl-- because we had one in Kozlovskii. In fact, now they are helping him. The society is helping him a lot. He's long dead. But his grandson is alive. And because his grandfather did so much and he was so honest to our people and so devoted, he used to bring them from the ghettos, stationed them in his farm, and bring them into the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Kozlovskii was a Pole?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- not-- a Belarus, better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Belarus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he spoke Yiddish like me and anybody did. He was born a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go at night. by auction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain people that knew how to-- to get food. It was like to going to battle. Because the Germans wanted food. The partisans wanted food. The Poles wanted food. Everybody want food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody have any guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without guns-- the younger people had guns. The guns were the main thing. Without a gun, you couldn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they got rid of the people that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --of the people that they knew from-- that wanted to take on them, you know-- to pay them off for what they did. Because Tovia's brothers, for the Russian, they were some kind of-- it was in the-- in the little village, they managed-- they-- something-- were involved in the communist party or-- and in the management or whatever. And so after, when the Germans came, they wanted to pay them off for it. So they were looking for him. They only wanted to get rid of the Bielskys. But some of them favored them, because my husband did quite a few good things. He saved one person's life when he was drowning. And his father and the son could not forget that. And then-- and-- and he had very good friends in the army, when he was in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those are people that he was one of them. His languages were so pure and he is so good, that nobody could even find out that he's a Jew in that time-- not by looks, and not by-- by speech. So some of them tried to bury them. Some of them tried to save them. And they knew the people. They grew up with those people. So they knew them. And slowly, slowly, first he started with the Nowogrodek ghetto, and then he started with the Lida ghetto. And he was carrying on terribly. I remember he was asked just recently, what would you like-- to kill 10 Germans or to save 1 Jew? And his answer was always, I want to save 1 Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was his main aim. And there's-- nobody came. Because from the sight of the rest of the people, they tried to save themselves. His big thing was that he tried to save others. He never thought. Because he could get the biggest job in any Russian brigade and be a big shot, and be a commander. But he wanted-- that's what he promised the people. That's what he wanted to accomplish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you prepare for the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the winter, they used to prepare a lot more. It was very hard, because the Germans found out that the peasants are giving food to the partisans too. And they-- and they restricted that. So sometimes you had to-- to take food by force. But they, for the winter, they prepared-- before we settled down, and we got to be what we were, it was not easy. And only the youngsters, the younger people, very brave people-- they loved my husband. They obeyed every rule and regulation. One thing he was screaming always-- if we won't have a discipline, we won't have any-- we won't survive. There is-- with Jews, it's very hard to manage, because we have different opinions. We have different ideas. And each one wants to be a commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he maintain the discipline?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tremendously-- just by love and devotion. He knew how to talk to them. I won't say that not once he had to raise his voice and say something what he wasn't supposed to say, but when he spoke or when he asked, it was like you could hear a fly passing by. The respect and the love to him was incredible. He was like God. If somebody said, if they'll go, and by him, they'll survive. If he won't be on the base and something will happen, we won't survive. That's how people believed in him-- like in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at the-- how old were you when you went there to the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '42--

speaker: interviewee
text: --16, close to 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did you do? What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- listen. In the beginning, I was nothing. What could you do? We weren't-- where we slept, we didn't eat, like I said before. We couldn't stay in one place, because-- or we were threatened. Or we heard news that the Germans are not far from us, and they will be close to us. And they were looking for us. So at night, the night was the day and the day was the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do to help the rest of the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in the beginning, not too much. But later, when we settled down on the-- on the raid basis, I was like any other soldier-- all-- not like a commander's wife. In the beginning, I wasn't the commander's wife. I was a commander's niece. But then in '43-- in '43, January 5th, my stepmother got very sick. She got the flu. And they took her to a farmer's place to recover a little bit, because she ran a very high temperature. Her sister and her son took her there. And it was a terrible raid on this house and two houses. They probably find out, the Germans, that they-- or the-- or the Poles or the Belarus, send out somebody to let them know, like they usually do. That's how my father got killed in the woods. And they-- and they sent probably somebody in, or they knew something. And they made a big raid on the house. And they were all killed alive. And this was January 1943-- January 5, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- we were stay-- we were on the base. We were in the woods, and they were in a house. So right away, we knew of what happened. We heard-- we heard even the shooting. It wasn't too far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was-- what was Tovia's reaction to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very upset for a while-- very upset and very depressed. But one thing he told me-- don't worry about it. You won't be-- now you're-- you're all alone, complete. I won't leave you alone. I'll always take care on you. Where I'll be, you'll be. I was crazy about him. I was absolutely crazy about him, because he had a way of talking to-- giving to me. A little warm was a lot. A little compassion was like giving me to eat after-- after weeks not eating. You know, I was hungry for it. And he knew how to do it. He cared. But my father was very smart. He saw our relationship. And he said to me, one day before he got killed-- he got killed '43, in March in 1943 too. He said to me, you know, I don't want to teach you, he said. But your relationship with Tovia is not like a family relationship-- not like a uncle, not like a-- a nephew. Your relationship is more than that. But remember, you'll break the-- you'll break the doors. You'll go from hand to the head. I'll never forget that, what he said to me. Because I see that it's not a normal relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And remember, watch yourself. It was very easy to get off track in-- in the woods, you know? You had to turn to somebody. You had to-- you wanted something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when-- when it came to food, I wasn't neglected. Because Tovia always got for me, even when his wife-- when his girlfriend was alive. But when it came to-- to be a human being, he had it, and they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Were-- was-- was there anyone else who was interested in you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty of people were interested, but I wasn't interested in nobody after them. And after my father died, he completely says-- after my father went, my father wanted to take revenge, because my-- his wife was killed. Usually Tovia never let him go, because in their eyes, he was a older man. And they didn't-- they wanted to spare him. And this was another story, too, a episode. I was sitting-- and he-- he wanted to go very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go where?

speaker: interviewee
text: For some kind of a-- they went for food, or they went for some-- for some action that the-- a group of partisans were sent from the-- from the brigade to-- to do at night. And he always wanted to be one of them. But Tovia felt that he's-- comparing with them, they were in the early 20s, and he's 47, 49-- no, he was 49 already when he was killed. He should sit on the base. He has nowhere to go, because he won't do too much. And he was very obnoxious about it. And he always wanted to go. But most of the time he didn't let him. This time, it was absolutely no words and no strength to stop him. He said he's going no matter what. If Tovia would want to start up with him, he could stop him. But he respected him too much. And he didn't want-- he was so anxious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting at the-- at the fire-- bond? Camp fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp fire. That was the only place to warm up-- and talking. And-- and he-- and I still tried to said-- I said, dad, look. Not too much-- too many are left of us-- me and you. Why don't you want-- you want to leave me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it's not a-- a secure thing to go on a-- on a search mission, like the-- our guys are going. Sometimes you come back. Sometimes you don't. Do you have a heart to leave me alone in this world?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't answer. And I thought maybe-- maybe I influenced him not to go. He got up, and he went away. The group got ready, you know, to go. And all of a sudden, he's coming back. And I was sitting. My tears was coming out. I was crying like a baby. I said, look. Even my own father doesn't want to listen to me. And he's coming. And I said, you see dad, blood is not water. You came back. He says, no. I didn't come back. I made up my mind, and I'm going. But I want-- I-- usually when I'm going away, I kiss you. This time, you were talking so much to me and influencing me, that I didn't have a chance to say goodbye to you. So I came back. The boys are waiting. He kissed me on my forehead. He said good-bye. And I never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do you know-- what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 15, 1943. Ten boys were killed-- a peasant farmer that worked-- a collaborator-- worked for the-- with the Germans. He sent out his son and let them know. He said they made-- they made the mission-- what they had to do. And they had for-- they packed a lot of food. And-- and it got, like I said before, the day was the night and the night was the day. So it got too-- too light. And they were afraid to move on the roads in order not to get involved with the Germans. They shouldn't see them, because then they will be you killed for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they decided to stay by the farmer in the house. And they put out people on-- you know, to watch them. What do you call this? Guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Guards. They put out guards. And the rest of them took a nap, because the whole night they worked. Meantime, this guy send out his younger son to the police and let them know. He came-- they came right away. And all of them, they killed. One hid himself under the stove. They had where the chickens used to be. He climbed over there, and he-- he was hiding. At night, when they were sitting, and-- and discussing what a big deal they did-- what a wonderful thing they did for the Germans, how devoted they were to the Germans-- to the German army-- he came out from underneath. And he says to them, I don't think this will be forgotten. Wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to stop at the end of the tape.
